notes.anchor: Had the trump of judgment blown
notes.note: The trump of judgment is the trumpet that, in Christian belief, sounds the Last Judgment and raises the dead (1 Corinthians 15:52; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). Ishmael reaches for it to measure the shock of Fedallah's midnight cry: the spout of an unseen whale jolts every sleeping sailor as violently as the trumpet of the apocalypse.

Noah Webster compiled the first great American dictionary (1828), the ancestor of the 1913 Webster's used in this edition. Ishmael's joke that neither Dr. Johnson's nor Noah Webster's dictionary holds the word gam shows the term belongs wholly to spoken whaling life, beneath the notice of formal lexicography.

notes.anchor: Like Mark Antony, for days and days along his green-turfed, flowery Nile
notes.note: The image is from Shakespeare's Antony and Cleopatra, where Mark Antony throws over his Roman duty to drift idly on the Nile with the Egyptian queen. Ishmael lays it over the canal-boatman lounging with his girl on the Erie Canal, making the Canaller a cut-rate, inland version of the Roman general's famous sensual idleness.

notes.anchor: by great odds, Beale's is the best
notes.note: Thomas Beale's Natural History of the Sperm Whale (1839) was the fullest scientific and practical study of the animal available to Melville, who leaned on it throughout the book. Beale, a surgeon who sailed on whalers, drew his engravings from creatures he had watched himself, giving them an accuracy that armchair naturalists could not match.

notes.anchor: the best outline pictures are in Scoresby
notes.note: William Scoresby's Account of the Arctic Regions (1820) was the standard work on the Greenland right-whale fishery, with anatomical plates drawn from whales he had measured in the field. Ishmael grants Scoresby's outlines their accuracy while complaining that the engravings are far too small to convey the animal's real scale.

notes.anchor: vast meadows of brit, the minute, yellow substance
notes.note: Brit is the sailor's name for the tiny crustaceans, chiefly copepods, that right whales strain from the sea through their baleen. Scoresby describes these yellow surface swarms in the Arctic and their importance as the chief food of the right and bowhead whales, the very creatures the Pequod sees feeding around it here.

notes.anchor: that wondrous Venetian blind in their mouths
notes.note: The Venetian blind is baleen, the long flexible plates of keratin hanging in parallel rows from the upper jaw of right and bowhead whales. As NOAA describes, the whale swims open-mouthed through dense brit and then pushes the water back out through the plates, trapping the tiny prey inside, a filter feeder on the grandest scale.

notes.anchor: under the feet of Korah and his company the live ground opened and swallowed them up forever
notes.note: In Numbers 16 Korah leads a revolt against Moses, and God makes the earth split open and swallow him, his household, and his followers alive. Ishmael takes it as the biblical type of sudden, total engulfment, then argues that the sea performs the same swallowing-up routinely, with no miracle announced.

notes.anchor: only as the Anak of the tribe
notes.note: The Anakim were a race of giants in Numbers 13:33, beside whom the Israelite spies felt themselves grasshoppers. Calling the giant squid the Anak of the tribe means the towering exemplar of its kind: the squid is to ordinary cuttlefish what a son of Anak was to an ordinary man.

The loggerhead is a stout post in the whaleboat's stern used to brake the whale-line. Once a whale is fast, the line is thrown round it; as Beale explains, the harpooneer adds or eases turns to control the strain, which is why the rope smokes and chars with friction as a struck whale tears the boat through the water.

Gay Head, now Aquinnah, is the western headland of Martha's Vineyard, home to the Wampanoag people, who supplied many skilled men to the whale fishery from the early 1700s on. Starbuck's history records Gay Head men sailing as harpooneers out of Nantucket and New Bedford, the very role Tashtego, the Gay-Header, holds aboard the Pequod.

notes.anchor: from the spiracle of the whale
notes.note: The spiracle is the sperm whale's single nostril, or blowhole, set at the left front of the head rather than on top as in right whales. Beale describes it closely: the jet of warm, moist breath forced through this one opening is the spout, and its sound and rising vapor were the whalemen's chief means of spotting whales far off.

notes.anchor: the headsman or whale-killer as temporary steersman
notes.note: In American whaling the headsman, the officer who commanded the boat and killed the whale with the lance, and the harpooneer traded places after the first iron was thrown, the harpooneer going aft to steer. Beale describes this switch, and Ishmael's complaint here, that it exhausts the very man needed for the kill, echoes a real dispute among whalers.

notes.anchor: It is a notched stick of a peculiar form
notes.note: The crotch is a forked wooden rest fixed in the bow of the whaleboat that holds the second, spare harpoon ready to throw. Beale shows it among his plates of whaling gear; keeping a second iron loaded in the crotch gave the harpooneer an instant follow-up if the first dart drew out or failed to hold.

In 2 Kings 2:11 the prophet Elijah does not die but is carried straight to heaven in a whirlwind, with a chariot of fire and horses of fire. Stubb's gibe, asking whether Fleece expects to be fetched like Elijah in a coach and four, teases the old cook for a plain, bodily faith in being lifted up to heaven.

Et tu, Brute, meaning and you, Brutus, are the dying words of Caesar in Shakespeare's Julius Caesar (3.1), the cry of a man stabbed by a trusted friend. Ishmael puts them in the mouth of a calf's head staring up at the diner who eats its brain, turning the meal into a grim act of self-betrayal against one's own kind.
